übertragen werden.

Systeme, die diese Informationen als Eingangsdaten nutzen, können so zeitnah Aussagen, beispielsweise über
den aktuellen Zustand eines Verkehrssystems, liefern.
Im Folgenden ist eine Übersicht der **gebräuchlichsten Übertragungstechnologien** zusammengestellt,
[aufgeschlüsselt nach maximal zu erreichender Datenrate und den entstehenden Kosten [Los18]:](http://forschungsinformationssystem.de:443/servlet/is/508300/)


[Auszug aus http://www.forschungsinformationssystem.de vom 12.02.2026](http://www.forschungsinformationssystem.de) Seite 1


Synthesebericht: Techniken der Datenübertragung für Intelligente Verkehrssysteme EintragsID: 339652


Die **leitungsgebundene Übertragung** bietet insbesondere durch die Verwendung von Glasfasertechnologie hohe
Datenübertragungsraten. Allerdings ist der Aufwand für das Verlegen von Leitungen und die damit verbundenen
Kosten hoch. Werden bereits bestehende Infrastrukturkomponenten benutzt, beispielsweise öffentliche Internetbasierte Netze, so fallen die Kosten deutlich geringer aus. Ein besonderer Fokus muss hier jedoch auf das
Sicherheitsmanagement gelegt werden. Dies kann durch Einrichtung eines Virtuellen Privaten Netzwerks (VPN)
erfolgen.

Die **drahtlose Übertragung** bietet sich für mobile, sogenannte "Floating Car Data" (FCD), oder temporär
installierte Sensorik sowie zur drahtlosen Anbindung von Sensoren an Streckenstationen an und lässt sich
meist kostengünstiger installieren, da keine Leitungen verlegt oder gemietet werden müssen. Nachteile sind
die freie Zugänglichkeit des Netzes, die Einschränkung der Reichweite der Funkübertragung sowie deren
Beeinflussung durch vorherrschende Umfeldbedingungen. Prinzipiell kann jeder, der ein entsprechendes
Empfangsgerät besitzt "mithören", was auf der Leitung passiert. Ein funktionierendes Sicherheitsmanagement ist


[Auszug aus http://www.forschungsinformationssystem.de vom 12.02.2026](http://www.forschungsinformationssystem.de) Seite 2


Synthesebericht: Techniken der Datenübertragung für Intelligente Verkehrssysteme EintragsID: 339652


daher Grundvoraussetzung für den sicheren Betrieb sowie ein umfangreicheres Fehlermanagement, um mögliche
Störungen erkennen und beheben zu können.

Bei den bisher aufgeführten Übertragungstechnologien handelt es sich meist um Technologien zur Punkt-zuPunkt-Verbindung zweier Kommunikationspartner. Es ist aber möglich nach weiteren Kommunikationsformen und
Verbindungsarten zu unterscheiden. Als **Kommunikationsformen** bieten sich an:


 Unicast - ein Kommunikationspartner kommuniziert mit genau einem anderen


 Broadcast - ein Kommunikationspartner kommuniziert mit beliebig vielen Kommunikationspartnern


 Geocast - ein Kommunikationspartner kommuniziert mit allen Kommunikationspartnern innerhalb einer
[festgelegten geografischen Region [StCo22]](http://forschungsinformationssystem.de:443/servlet/is/503888/)


Weiterhin unterscheidet man die folgende **Verbindungsarten:**


 unidirektionale Verbindung - genau ein Sender und genau ein Empfänger; der Kommunikationspartner ist
entweder Sender oder Empfänger


 bidirektionale Verbindung - ein Sender, ein Empfänger; jeder Kommunikationspartner kann gleichzeitig sowohl
[Sender als auch Empfänger sein [JuWa02]](http://forschungsinformationssystem.de:443/servlet/is/503889/)


"Das **Vehicular Ad-Hoc Network** (VANet) stellt ein besonderes Ad-hoc-Netzwerk zur Fahrzeug-zu-Fahrzeug(C2C) und Fahrzeug-zu-Infrastruktur-Kommunikation (C2I) mittels "Wireless Local Area Network" (WLAN) dar. Es
ist speziell auf die Anforderungen der sicheren Kommunikation bei hoher Geschwindigkeit und die Kommunikation
in hoch dynamischen Umgebungen ausgelegt; hohe Datenankunftsraten und geringe Verzögerungszeiten sind ein
[herausragendes Merkmal [Hart10]."](http://forschungsinformationssystem.de:443/servlet/is/339658/)
Die Kommunikation zwischen Kommunikationspartnern kann direkt (One-Hop) oder indem ein oder mehrere
Fahrzeuge als Zwischenstation dienen (Multi-Hop) stattfinden. Um die Kommunikationsreichweite eines Fahrzeugs
zu vergrößern, können an festgelegten Punkten sogenannte "Intelligent Transport System (ITS) Road Side
Stations (IRS)" aufgestellt werden.

Unabhängig von der verwendeten Hardware werden (auf den oberen Schichten des ISO/OSI-Referenzmodells,

[[ISO7489]) bestimmte](http://forschungsinformationssystem.de:443/servlet/is/339656/) **Schnittstellen beziehungsweise Protokolle** zur Datenübertragung auf den

**Glossar**


**Ad-hoc-Netz** Kommunikationsnetze oder -netzwerke, die sich nur für die Dauer der
Kommunikation aufbauen. Zwei oder mehr Endgeräte verbinden sich zu einem
vermaschten Telekommunikationsnetz. Diese Netze bauen sich selbständig auf
und konfigurieren sich selbständig.

**Floating Car Data**
Bei Floating Car Data (FCD) geht es um das Messen und Auswerten
der Fahrzeugbewegungen anhand von Daten, die direkt aus dem
Fahrzeug kommen. Grundlage ist Global Positioning System (GPS) für
die Positionsbestimmung im Fahrzeug und Global System for Mobile
Communication (GSM)-Kommunikation für die Übertragung von Informationen
zwischen Fahrzeug und Zentrale. Damit entsteht im Fahrzeug eine
mitschwimmende Messstation für Verkehrsdaten.

In der Zentrale werden die eingehenden anonymisierten Meldungen temporär
zwischengespeichert und deren Integrität geprüft. Mit Hilfe von digitalen
Karten kann damit der Fahrweg und das Fahrprofil rekonstruiert werden. Die
aufbereiteten Daten fließen in ein Rechnersystem zur Zustandsmodellierung
der Verkehrslage. Daraus lassen sich aktuelle Verkehrsmeldungen,

reference:
Die **Vehicle-to-X-Kommunikation (V2X)** ist eine zentrale Technologie für die Vernetzung von Fahrzeugen mit ihrer Umgebung und umfasst verschiedene Kommunikationsarten: **Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V)**, **Vehicle-to-Infrastructure (V2I)**, **Vehicle-to-Pedestrian (V2P)** und **Vehicle-to-Network (V2N)**. Ihre Kernfunktionen lassen sich in den Bereichen **Sicherheit, Verkehrsflussoptimierung, Umweltentlastung und Informationsbereitstellung** zusammenfassen.

Die wichtigste Funktion ist die **Erhöhung der Verkehrssicherheit**. V2V ermöglicht den direkten Austausch von Position, Geschwindigkeit, Fahrtrichtung und Bremszuständen zwischen Fahrzeugen. Dadurch können frühzeitig Gefahren erkannt werden, z. B. ein plötzlich bremsendes Fahrzeug, ein Unfall oder ein Hindernis auf der Fahrbahn, selbst wenn diese noch außerhalb der Sichtlinie liegen. V2P erweitert dies auf Fußgänger oder Radfahrer, die über Smartphones oder Wearables signalisieren können, wo sie sich befinden.

Ein weiterer Kernbereich ist die **Optimierung des Verkehrsflusses**. V2I-Kommunikation ermöglicht Ampeln, Straßen- und Mautsystemen, auf Echtzeitinformationen der Fahrzeuge zu reagieren, etwa Grünphasen dynamisch anzupassen oder Staus zu reduzieren. V2N erlaubt die Integration in **Cloud- und Flottenmanagementsysteme**, um Verkehrs- und Routeninformationen global zu nutzen.

Darüber hinaus unterstützt V2X **energieeffizientes Fahren**. Fahrzeuge können über Routeninformationen, Verkehrsdichte oder Geschwindigkeitsempfehlungen Kraftstoffverbrauch und Emissionen minimieren.

Technisch basiert V2X auf **DSRC (Dedicated Short Range Communication)** oder **C-V2X (Cellular V2X)**, die niedrige Latenzen, hohe Zuverlässigkeit und Priorisierung sicherheitskritischer Daten gewährleisten.

Zusammengefasst erfüllt V2X die Kernfunktionen **Sicherheitswarnungen, Verkehrsoptimierung, Energieeffizienz und Informationsaustausch**, wodurch sie eine Schlüsselrolle für vernetztes, kooperatives und teilweise autonomes Fahren spielt.

user_input:
Welche Voraussetzungen/Faktoren müssen für einen Transfer von Technologie Wayside-Sensorik (Streckenseitige Sensoren) in Bereich Rolling
Stock in Motion erfüllt sein ?
response:
Erforderliche Messparameter und Systemfunktionen
Um den Zustand von Fahrzeugen im laufenden Betrieb („in Motion“) effektiv zu überwachen, muss das System folgende Faktoren identifizieren und bewerten können:

Heißläufer- und Bremsüberwachung: Erkennung von überhitzten Achslagern (hot boxes) sowie erhitzten Rad- oder Scheibenbremsen.

Raddefekte: Identifizierung von Flachstellen, Unrundheiten oder Polygonisierungen an den Rädern.

Dynamisches Wiegen: Erfassung des Gewichts und der Lastverteilung der Fahrzeuge in Echtzeit.

Wirtschaftliche und betriebliche Faktoren
Für eine erfolgreiche Implementierung und einen ökonomischen Betrieb nennt das Papier folgende Voraussetzungen:

Kosteneffizienz: Das System muss dazu beitragen, die Wartungskosten für das Rollmaterial und die Infrastruktur zu senken.


Unfallprävention: Die vorzeitige Erkennung von Problemen im Fahrwerk ist entscheidend, um die Sicherheit zu verbessern und Entgleisungen zu verhindern.


Einnahmenoptimierung: Durch die Kontrolle von Gewicht und Lastverteilung in Echtzeit kann ein wirtschaftlicher Effekt durch die bessere Erhebung von Transportgebühren und Steuern erzielt werden.

Technische Umsetzung (Sensoren)
Technisch basiert das System auf spezialisierten Sensoren, wie beispielsweise:


Kraft-Sensoren (Force Sensors): Zur Messung der dynamischen Einwirkungen der Räder auf die Schiene.


Infrarot-Überwachung: Einsatz von Wärmebildkameras (z. B. FLIR), um Alarme bei überhitzten Rädern oder Bremsen auszulösen.


Identifikationssysteme: Nutzung von RFID-AVI-Systemen (wie „RAIL-READER“), um die Fahrzeuge während der Fahrt eindeutig zu identifizieren und zu tracken.

["# **Sensors and Microsystems of Wayside Monitoring System for Rolling** **Stock in Motion** Nencho Nenov [1)], Emil Dimitrov [2)], Nikolay Dodev [1)] and Vasil Vasilev [1) ] 1) Department of Transport Equipment, University of Transport, Sofia, Bulgaria 2) Department of Electronic Technology, Technical University of Sofia, Bulgaria nnenov_58@abv.bg _**Abstract**_ _:_ _The paper presented is dedicated to the development and application of sensors and_ _microsystems for evaluating various parameters of rolling stock in motion. The intended purpose of_ _such a system, comprising of the described sensors and microsystems and also known as Wayside_ _Monitoring System for Rolling Stock in Motion, is to identify the current overall condition of the_ _railway vehicles, including hot boxes (axle bearings) and brakes (wheel brakes and disc brakes),_ _wheel defects (flat spots, out of roundness, polygonization, etc), dynamic weighing and load_ _distribution, etc. All of these could compromise the safety of the railroad traffic, leading to accidents_ _such as vehicle derailment and increased wear and damages of the tracks._ **1.** **INTRODUCTION** The working condition under which the railway vehicles operate could be very severe. This in most cases includes innumerable elements and activities that have a detrimental effect on its overall condition and potentially on the safety of passengers and freight. The capacity and speeds of the rolling stock also increases. In order to be cost effective, the railway traffic should be running smooth and free of accidents and unscheduled track maintenance. In order to evaluate the overall condition of the rolling stock without interrupting the railway work, the research team is currently developing sensors and microsystems of wayside monitoring system for the needs of the Bulgarian National Railway Infrastructure Company (NRIC). The monitoring system will be also used by the railway operators and haulers in order to track their vehicles and loads. Another intended purpose of the system is the economic effect by increasing the collection of freight charges of overloaded vehicles. **2.** **BASIC PRINCIPLES AND APPROACHES FOR** **MODELLING OF THE SYSTEM** The modelling process and building of a wayside monitoring system includes many factors that need to be taken into account, such as: basic functions of the monitoring system, choosing the location of the check-points, selecting the right method for vehicle identification, selecting appropriate measuring methods and sensors, defining procedures and algorithms about each local check-point, processing statistical information about alarms, etc. In order the information obtained through the monitoring system to be useful for real-time decision making and the vehicles and loads to be tracked, the local check-points should be transferring data to control centers. Taking into account the local conditions and the remoteness of a check-point, there are basically two methods for transferring data – by the fiber-optic network of NRIC (wired) or by the GSM-R technology (wirelessly). The locations of the check-points throughout the railway in Bulgaria are shown on Fig. 1. For reasons of economy and efficiency the wayside monitoring system in Bulgaria will feature the following functions: 1) Identification of a vehicle by its individual number; 2) Measuring the load of each wheel – detection of overload, unbalanced vehicles and uneven load distribution between the wheels and axles; 3) Detection of wheel defects – flat spots, out of roundness, polygonizaition, etc.; 4) Detection of hot boxes, wheels and brakes; 5) Telecommunication between the local check-points and control centers. **3.** **SENSORS AND MICROSYSTEMS** Each of the check-points (Fig. 1) has been individually equipped with sensors and microsystems, depending on its designed features. Basically every check-point needs to be featuring vehicle identification and telecommunication. But these two features alone could not deliver useful information about some of the parameters of the passing trains. This demands at least one more feature to be added, e.g. detection of wheel load, detection of wheel defects or detection of hot boxes, wheels and brakes. _**3.1. Vehicle identification microsystem**_ The vehicle identification microsystem is crucial for the wayside monitoring system as all of the measured parameters of the passing train are linked to each vehicle of it. For that reason, such a microsystem should have the ability to work reliably at any environment conditions. The microsystem should also be able to accurately read the vehicle’s identification numbers, plates, signs, etc. During the decision making about what type of identification method should the microsystems is best to use, the research team had made several trials of different available systems for optical character recognition (OCR). The results were unsatisfactory for several reason. The major effect on the OCR was that the identification numbers, plates, signs, etc. of the vehicles are not kept in good condition. Most of the freight vehicles were covered with dirt, rust, etc. The speed of the passing train was also a problem for accurate OCR. Adding the weather conditions, such as raining, snowing, sun light, etc., that made the OCR method for vehicles identification inapplicable. Based on the results from the OCR trials, the research team has decided to use radio frequency devices to transmit data between each other as the train passes the check-point – a Rail-TAG and a RailREADER. The Rail-TAG represents an electronic device, mounted on the vehicle, which stores the vehicles identification data – Fig. 2 [1]. The specifications of the Rail-TAG transponder are listed in Table 1 [1]. **Fig. 2.** Rail-TAG transponder, mounted on a wagon. **Fig. 3.** Rail-READER, mounted near the track. The Rail-READER – Fig. 3 [1] – is a wayside electronic device, which reads the data, transmitted by the Rail-TAG. The Rail-READER is the device, connected to the local check-point infrastructure. The specifications of the Rail-READER are listed in Table 2 [1]. The on-track equipment, depending on the client request, may include several measurement microsystems. All of them provide data to the road side equipment, which comprises a computer system for data acquisition and data processing. The measurement results are then transferred to the information user through local networks or Internet by using the XML Protocol. The information provided by the wayside monitoring system is used by the railway operators, haulers, railway signaling system, etc. in order to track and evaluate the condition of the passing vehicles. As mentioned above, the initiation of the measuring process of all the microsystems is triggered by rail contacts. They represent simple strain-gauge force sensors, installed on the track before and after the check-point zone. Their signal is used by the roadside equipment for triggering the process for the ontrack equipment. The axle counting is crucial for identification and linking the measured data to the corresponding vehicle and its axles. After the last axle in the train has passed the rail contact, installed after the check-point zone, the data measuring process is stopped and the acquired data is processed by the road-side equipment. Depending on the train length, the processing time may vary, but no more than couple of minutes. After the processing has been completed, the roadside equipment sends the results, along with alarms, remarks, etc. to the information users by the local network (fiber-optics) or Internet (wirelessly through GSM-R technology). In order to minimize the amount of data to be transferred, the system uses the XML Protocol. In case of power interruption, the system is backed up by alternative power supply (UPS) for couple of hours before complete shutdown. Der **Transfer von Wayside-Sensorik** in den Bereich **Rolling Stock in Motion** erfordert die Anpassung streckenseitiger Technologien, damit sie direkt auf fahrenden Zügen oder mobilen Einheiten eingesetzt werden können. Zentrale Voraussetzungen betreffen **Sensorik, Datenverarbeitung, Kommunikationsinfrastruktur und Sicherheitsanforderungen**.

Erstens müssen die Sensoren **mobil- und vibrationsresistent** sein. Während Wayside-Sensoren fest installiert sind, sind Rolling-Stock-Sensoren kontinuierlich mechanischen Erschütterungen, Temperaturschwankungen, Feuchtigkeit und elektromagnetischen Störungen ausgesetzt. Daher müssen sie robust konstruiert und präzise kalibriert sein, um unter dynamischen Bedingungen verlässliche Messungen zu liefern.

Zweitens ist die **Echtzeit-Datenverarbeitung** entscheidend. An Bord von Zügen können Daten nicht zentral gesammelt werden, wie es bei stationären Sensoren möglich ist. Es werden daher leistungsfähige Edge-Computing- oder Embedded-Systeme benötigt, die Sensordaten lokal analysieren, aggregieren und gegebenenfalls direkt für Steuerungs- oder Sicherheitsentscheidungen bereitstellen.

Drittens ist die **Datenübertragung** ein kritischer Faktor. Rolling-Stock-Systeme müssen sicherstellen, dass gemessene Daten zuverlässig an Leitstellen oder Cloud-Plattformen übertragen werden, entweder über Funknetze, 5G, LTE-Rail oder über Bord-Speicher mit späterer Synchronisation. QoS-Anforderungen wie geringe Latenz und hohe Verfügbarkeit müssen eingehalten werden, insbesondere für sicherheitskritische Funktionen.

Viertens müssen **Standards, Interoperabilität und Zertifizierungen** berücksichtigt werden. Sensoren müssen mit Bordnetzen, Steuerungssystemen und Zug-Management-Systemen kompatibel sein und regulatorische Anforderungen erfüllen, etwa hinsichtlich SIL (Safety Integrity Level) oder EN 50126/50128/50129.

Insgesamt erfordert der Transfer von Wayside- zu Rolling-Stock-Sensorik **technische Robustheit, Echtzeitverarbeitung, zuverlässige Kommunikation und Einhaltung von Sicherheits- und Interoperabilitätsstandards**, um präzise, kontinuierliche und sichere Datenerfassung während der Zugfahrt zu gewährleisten.

user_input: Welchen Einfluss haben verschiedene Arten von Signalstörungen und spezifische Geländeprofile (wie Berge oder Tunnel) auf die Genauigkeit der GNSS-Positionierung, und wie lässt sich dies mathematisch bewerten?
response: Analyse der Positionierungsleistung unter RealbedingungenDas Dokument untersucht die Leistungsfähigkeit von GNSS-basierten Zugpositionierungssystemen mithilfe von Colored Petri Nets (CPNs), um die dynamischen Interaktionen zwischen Signalen und Umweltfaktoren systematisch abzubilden.+11. Mathematische Bewertung des PositionierungsfehlersZur Bewertung der Systemleistung wird der Fehlerabstand zwischen der berechneten Position (aus den GNSS-Beobachtungen) und einer Referenzposition (Ground Truth) ermittelt. Dies geschieht mithilfe der Euklidischen Distanzformel im dreidimensionalen Raum:+1$$d=\sqrt{(x_{2}-x_{1})^{2}+(y_{2}-y_{1})^{2}+(z_{2}-z_{1})^{2}}$$.Hierbei stehen $(x_{1}, y_{1}, z_{1})$ für die Referenzkoordinaten und $(x_{2}, y_{2}, z_{2})$ für die berechneten Koordinaten.2. Einfluss von Signalstörungen (Interferenzen)Die Studie zeigt, dass Signalstörungen die Genauigkeit im Vergleich zum Normalzustand (Fehler ca. 1,03 m) massiv verschlechtern:FM-Signale (Frequenzmodulation): Diese verursachen mit einem mittleren Fehler von 6,22 m die schwerwiegendsten Beeinträchtigungen. Sie führen zu Frequenzverschiebungen, die den Doppler-Effekt verstärken und insbesondere die Fehler in Ost- und Nordrichtung erhöhen.+2AM-Signale (Amplitudenmodulation): Diese erhöhen den mittleren Fehler auf 4,95 m. Da sie die Signalstärke direkt beeinflussen, reagiert die vertikale Komponente (Höhe) besonders empfindlich auf diese Schwankungen.+1Puls-Signale: Diese periodischen, intensiven Stöße führen zu einem Fehler von 4,79 m. Sie destabilisieren den Signalempfang in allen drei räumlichen Dimensionen.+13. Einfluss der BetriebsumgebungDie Umgebung spielt eine entscheidende Rolle für die Signalverfügbarkeit und -qualität:Gebirgiges Gelände (Mountain Occlusion): Der Fehler steigt auf ca. 1,30 m an. Dies resultiert aus Signalabschirmungen und Mehrwegeeffekten (Multipath), die durch das komplexe Terrain verursacht werden.Tunnel-Szenarien: Hier ist die Degradation mit einem Fehler von 5,67 m am stärksten.Im Tunnel: Das Signal ist vollständig blockiert, was zum Verlust der Positionierungsfähigkeit führt.+1Tunnelausgang: Unmittelbar nach dem Verlassen des Tunnels treten durch Signalreakquisition und starke Reflexionen zunächst sehr hohe Fehlerwerte auf, die sich erst mit zunehmender Entfernung vom Tunnel stabilisieren.

Shuting Chen [a][,][b], Daohua Wu [a][,][b][,][⁎], Jiang Liu [a][,][c][,][d], Siqi Wang [a][,][d ]

_Keywords:_

**1. Introduction**



A B S T R A C T


Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS)-based continuous and accurate train positioning is one of the key
technologies for advanced train operations such as train virtual coupling. However, GNSS-based train posi­
tioning faces significant challenges in real-world scenarios due to environmental complexities and signal in­
terferences. Considering this issue, this paper presents an approach for modeling and performance analysis of
GNSS-based train positioning systems using Colored Petri Nets (CPNs). By systematically modeling the GNSS
signal reception and processing process, the performance of the positioning system under various environment
scenarios is evaluated. The system model integrates three types of interference signals (i.e., Amplitude
Modulation (AM) signals, Frequency Modulation (FM) signals, and pulse signals) while incorporating environ­
mental factors such as terrain obstructions and tunnel shielding. Additionally, the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)
algorithm is employed to process GNSS observation data, providing accurate train position estimations. The
simulation results demonstrate that signal interferences and complex environmental conditions significantly
affect the GNSS-based positioning accuracy. This study offers a comprehensive framework for evaluating the
performance of GNSS-based train positioning systems in different scenarios, highlighting critical factors that
influence positioning accuracy and stability.



With the increasing demand for operation safety and efficiency in
rail transportation, train virtual coupling has been proposed and be­
come a hot research topic in recent years. For virtual coupling, con­
tinuous and accurate train positioning is a cornerstone. Due to the
global coverage and high availability, Global Navigation Satellite
Systems (GNSSs) have been widely employed for train positioning [1].
However, GNSS-based positioning faces significant challenges in com­
plex operational environments such as mountainous regions and tun­
nels, where signal obstructions and interferences lead to accuracy de­
gradation.
Extensive researches have focused on the application of GNSS in
train positioning, specifically under idealized conditions with unin­
terrupted and unobstructed signal reception [2–4]. Existing methods,
such as Extended Kalman Filters (EKFs) and data fusion with inertial
sensors, have been demonstrated notable in improving positioning



accuracy [5,6]. However, these works lack a comprehensive perfor­
mance analysis of GNSS-based positioning in real-world operational
environments such as tunnels and mountainous terrains, where signal
interferences and obstructions occur frequently.
One of the critical challenges in applying GNSS-based train posi­
tioning is analyzing its performances under various operational condi­
tions including that GNSS signals are interfered with by interference
signals such as Amplitude Modulation (AM) signals, Frequency
Modulation (FM) signals, and pulse signals, or obstructed by mountains
and tunnels. Traditional approaches often fail to intuitively represent
the dynamic interactions of GNSS signals and the environmental effects
on positioning accuracy. To this end, this paper proposes a Colored
Petri Net (CPN)-based approach to analyze the performance of GNSSbased train positioning systems. CPNs provide a powerful framework to
describe the dynamic behaviors and concurrent processes of GNSS
signal reception, signal interferences, and environmental impacts of the
positioning systems. The objective of this study is to evaluate the

The main contributions of this paper are as follows.


1) A novel CPN-based modeling framework for GNSS-based train po­

sitioning systems is developed.
2) Dynamic influences of signal interferences (i.e., by AM signals, FM
signals, pulse signals) and environmental obstructions (i.e., by
mountains and tunnels) are incorporated into the model.
3) The performance of the GNSS-based train positioning system is
quantitatively evaluated under various scenarios with the CPN
model of the system.


**2. Related works**


Over the past decades, GNSS-based systems have been extensively ap­
plied to train positioning due to their global coverage and high accuracy
under favorable conditions [1,7]. Despite these advantages, challenges such
as signal interferences and terrain-induced occlusions continue to pose
questions on high positioning accuracy and stability. Numerous studies have
explored techniques to mitigate the impacts of signal interferences on GNSSbased positioning accuracy [8–12]. For example, Ref. [9] investigates the
impact of continuous wave interference on GNSS receiver tracking perfor­
mance, examining the extent of its influence by varying data bit durations
or integration times. To address interference-related challenges, an inter­
ference energy detection method and a comprehensive orientation tech­
nology tailored for GNSS are introduced in Ref. [11]. Besides, a number of
researches have examined the impact of environmental factors along
railway lines on GNSS signal integrity. For instance, Ref. [13] explores
methods of scenario-based weighted measurement noise calculation and
fault detection combined with state recognition based on integrity evalua­
tion in autonomous train positioning systems. Some studies have achieved
high positioning accuracy in partially obstructed or challenging environ­
ments by fusing multi-sensor data [6,14,15].
In contrast to studies that mainly investigate GNSS signal inter­
ferences in isolation, this paper integrates environmental factors and
signal interferences into the formal model of the positioning system. By
utilizing model simulation, the proposed approach enables a quantita­
tive evaluation of the system’s performance. This method provides a
systematic analysis and verification of the interactions among signal
interferences, environmental conditions, and train positioning.
Due to the rigorous mathematical foundation, formal methods have
gained recognition as effective tools for system modeling and



verification. In this work, we adopt CPNs [16] as the formal modeling
framework to study the impact of signal interferences and environ­
mental factors on GNSS-based train positioning. Petri nets are formal
modeling languages that are well-known for systems characterized as
concurrent, synchronous, distributed, etc. Some application examples
adopting petri nets as the means of description for railway signaling
systems can be found in Refs. [17–22].


**3. System modeling with CPNs**


Fig. 1 illustrates the modeling framework of a GNSS-based train
positioning system comprising the following main parts: GNSS Re­
ceiver, Environment Scenarios, Interference Signals, GNSS Position
Solution, and Evaluation Module. The module GNSS Receiver selects
specific environment scenarios and receives GNSS signals. The
module Environment Scenarios is composed of three submodules:
Open Area Scenario, Mountain Occlusion Scenario, and Tunnel and
Exit Tunnel Scenario. These submodules simulate various real-world
conditions while generating GNSS signal interferences, i.e., GNSS
signals are interfered with by AM, FM, and pulse signals. In the
module GNSS Positioning Solution, the EKF algorithm is employed to
process interfered GNSS signals for estimating the train’s position.
The module Evaluation compares the calculated position with the
reference position and outputs the positioning error. Fig. 2 clearly
illustrates the hierarchical relationships between modules of the CPN
model, providing a structured view of their layered organization. The
declarations of the model are categorized and summarized in two
tables for clarity. Table 1 provides the colset declarations and their
respective descriptions, while Table 2 provides the variable declara­
tions and their respective descriptions.


_3.1. Top-level of the positioning system model_


The top-level of the CPN model is shown in Fig. 3. Each substitution
transition corresponds to a specific component of the system archi­
tecture. The commands or feedbacks are represented by the tokens on
the places. The GNSS Receiver processes GNSS signals transmitted by
the satellite constellation. Using the EKF algorithm, the system per­
forms a position solution to determine the train’s position at the current
time. The calculated position coordinates are then evaluated against
reference coordinates for performance analysis. This evaluation assesses
the positioning accuracy of the GNSS-based train positioning system
under various environment scenarios and signal interferences.

modeling under different operational conditions. The transition Choose

_3.2.1. Submodule Open Area_
Fig. 5 illustrates the CPN model designed to simulate the impacts of
GNSS signal interferences on the observation signals. This model in­
troduces three typical types of interference signals, i.e., AM, FM, and
pulse signals. Boolean variables are used to control the activation of
each interference type. This design ensures that the generation process
of each interference signal is both independent and easily manageable.
Additionally, it accurately reflects the characteristics of realistic signal
interference environments.



For the transition AM Interference, amplitude-modulated inter­
ference signals are generated by multiplying a low-frequency sinusoidal
envelope (1 Hz) with a high-frequency carrier wave centered at
1575.42 MHz, corresponding to the GNSS L1 band. This configuration
produces a time-varying envelope with a modulation depth of 0.5, ef­
fectively simulating realistic amplitude fluctuations observed in Radio
Frequency (RF) interference sources. A similar signal interference set­
ting is illustrated in Ref. [23]. Dynamic amplitude variations can sig­
nificantly degrade GNSS signal reception, particularly in high-noise or
contested environments.
The transition FM Interference simulates frequency-modulated
signal interference by generating two sinusoidal components with
symmetrical frequency deviations around a carrier frequency centered
at 1575.42 MHz, corresponding to the GNSS L1 band. These deviations
are sampled from a Gaussian distribution with a standard deviation of
75 kHz, emulating the spectral characteristics of typical FM broadcast
signals [24]. This approach captures the dynamic spectral spreading
commonly observed in real-world RF interference.
The transition Pulse Interference generates periodic pulse signals to si­
mulate sudden and intense bursts of RF energy. It samples two random
parameters: the pulse width _T_ p which represents the duration of each pulse,
and the pulse interval _Ti_ which defines the spacing between successive
pulses. A uniformly distributed amplitude between 1 and 5 simulates the
varying severity of the interference. At each time step, the model checks
whether the current time modulo the total pulse period _T_ p + _Ti_ falls within
the pulse’s active duration. If so, an interference signal of the specified
amplitude is emitted; otherwise, it remains zero. This periodic pulsed
structure reflects typical burst-like interference patterns observed in GNSS
environments [25], characterized by sharp, short-lived increases in signal
strength followed by rapid attenuation.
The input data enter the module through the input places Open Area
and GNSS Signal, representing the GNSS satellites and the initial en­
vironmental conditions. The place Bool Control and transition Mutex
manage the logic for introducing signal interference, with the code in
Mutex ensuring that the generation processes for different interference
types do not conflict. Each interference signal is integrated into the
GNSS data by firing the transition Interference. The GNSS data affected
by signal interferences proceed to the observation output stage con­
trolled by the place GNSS Number. The final results are outputted from
the output place GNSS Observation.


_3.2.2. Submodule Mountain_
Fig. 6 illustrates the shielding effect of mountainous terrain on GNSS
signals. In addition to incorporating AM, FM, and pulse signal inter­
ferences, the module introduces environmental factors of mountain
height, terrain data, and obstruction distance. These elements are used



178


_S. Chen, D. Wu, J. Liu et al._ _High-speed Railway 3 (2025) 175–184_


**Fig. 5.** Submodule Open Area.



to filter out signals from satellites that are obstructed by the terrain. The
places Mountain Height and Distance store mountain elevations and
spatial relationships between the terrain and GNSS signal paths. These
serve as the foundational data for modeling and analyzing how terrain
features obstruct satellite signals. The transition Mountain Data Fusion
and its code are responsible for integrating and transmitting these data.
The place GNSS Signal stores the initial GNSS satellite data. The
transition Mountain Scenario integrates mountain height and obstruc­
tion distance information with current visible satellite data to filter out
obstructed signals. This process assesses satellite visibility by com­
paring each satellite’s elevation angle with the obstruction angle de­
rived from the mountain height and the receiver’s distance to the
mountain. If a satellite’s elevation angle is lower than the obstruction
angle, it is deemed as obstructed and excluded from the positioning
calculations. After the firing of the transition Mountain Scenario, the
GNSS data are stored in the place Data with Mountain Error.
Additionally, visible satellite data are combined with interference sig­
nals and output to the place GNSS Observation. By incorporating a
mountainous terrain scenario, this module effectively simulates the
dynamic impact of terrain obstructions on GNSS signal visibility in
complex environments.


_3.2.3. Submodule Tunnel_
Fig. 7 illustrates the process of GNSS signal reception and error variation
for a train in a tunnel scenario. It focuses on the dynamic impact of the
tunnel environment on the satellite signal visibility and positioning accu­
racy. In this module, the places InTunnel, Just Out, and Out Tunnel re­
present the train’s current state, including three phases: inside the tunnel,
just outside the tunnel, and completely outside the tunnel.



Inside the tunnel, a train is shielded by the structure blocking
GNSS signal reception. During this phase, the place GNSS Signal
remains empty indicating the complete obstruction of satellite sig­
nals. When the train just exits the tunnel, the GNSS signals become
visible again. However, due to the complex environmental condi­
tions near the tunnel exit, such as multipath effects, signal reflec­
tions, and delays, the GNSS observation data contain significant er­
rors. This process is modeled by the code of the transition Tunnel
Scenario, which integrates the tunnel state from the place Tunnel
with visible satellite information. Over time, as the GNSS signal re­
ception stabilizes, the positioning errors gradually decrease. This
dynamic adjustment is governed by the place Time Counter. The
place GNSS Observation outputs the GNSS data affected by the tunnel
scenario, including both the high-error initial data and the stabilized
high-accuracy data. This module effectively captures the three-phase
impact of tunnel scenarios on GNSS signal reception: complete signal
loss inside the tunnel, significant errors immediately after exiting,
and eventual signal stabilization outside the tunnel.


_3.3. Module GNSS Position Solution_


Fig. 8 illustrates the process of transforming GNSS observations into
train positions. GNSS observations, received from the GNSS Receiver
module, are sequentially processed by the transition EKF. The com­
pletion of the EKF process is indicated by the output flag bEKF=true,
which signals readiness for subsequent evaluation. Due to the limited
matrix computation capability of CPN, the tokens on the place Position
are generated with MATLAB. The pseudocode of the GNSS data pro­
cessing workflow and position solution is presented in Algorithm 1.

the module include selecting GNSS environment scenarios, initiating
GNSS data simulation, and calculating positioning errors. The module

The resulting error values are then output via the place Delta
Position. The environment scenario is dynamically selected through the
firing of the transition Select Environment Scenario. The coordinate
values stored on the place Reference Position originate from an actual
train trajectory, representing a segment of real-world train movement.
This dataset serves as a benchmark and is precisely aligned with the

**4. Simulation and analysis results**


To ensure the correctness of the CPN model, formal verification is
essential. Table 3 presents part of the standard state space report for the
developed model. The state space is complete, comprising 5365 mark­
ings and 6410 arcs. The Strongly Connected Component (SCC) graph
contains the same number of nodes and arcs, indicating the absence of
cycles in the state space. The verification was performed using five
representative input datasets, which cover all potential operational
paths of the system. This ensures that the generated state space fully
captures the range of behaviors that the model can exhibit under dif­
ferent input conditions. The home properties show that the initial
marking is not a home marking. According to the liveness properties,
the model includes 648 dead markings, but no dead or live transition
instances. The fairness properties confirm that there are no infinite



occurrence sequences. These findings together demonstrate the model’s
soundness, boundedness, and finite execution. The existence of dead
markings suggests that certain process branches reach terminal states
where no further transitions are possible. This behavior is consistent
with the expected functioning of the train positioning system, where a
positioning cycle may conclude or the process may terminate early due
to data filtering or error detection.
To simulate train positioning, loops are added to the model. Each
unit of time in the model corresponds to one second in the real world. A
simulation running for 600-time units represents ten minutes of station
operation. To facilitate data analysis, a breakpoint monitor is set to halt
the simulation when the model time exceeds 600-time units.
The dataset used for the simulation is based on a segment of the
actual Jing–Shen high-speed railway. Track coordinates are derived
from field measurements, while satellite ephemeris data are obtained
from standard RINEX broadcast ephemeris files. At each observation
epoch along the track, visible satellites and their corresponding signal
parameters are calculated. Specifically, satellite positions are de­
termined using broadcast ephemeris data through standard GNSS or­
bital propagation models. True pseudoranges are computed based on
the geometric distance between the satellite and the receiver, assuming
ideal signal propagation without any observation errors. Azimuth and
elevation angles are computed by transforming the satellite coordinates
into the local horizon coordinate system relative to the receiver’s po­
sition. These computations adhere to established GNSS observation

Under interference-free conditions, the positioning system demon­
strates high accuracy and stability, with a mean error of 1.0279 m and a
standard deviation of 0.0552 m. However, under signal interference
conditions, the system’s performance degrades significantly. The AM
signal interference increases the mean error to 4.9484 m and the stan­
dard deviation to 4.0833 m, with the elevation direction showing the
most significant fluctuations. This is likely due to the AM signal inter­
ference directly affecting signal power through amplitude variations,
leading to enhanced vertical errors. The FM signal interference causes
even more severe impacts, with the mean error rising to 6.2241 m and
the standard deviation of 5.2630 m. The FM signal interference in­
troduces frequency shifts exacerbating Doppler effects and significantly
increasing error fluctuations in the eastward and northward directions.
The pulse signal interference characterized by periodic bursts of highintensity disruption, results in a mean error of 4.7925 m and a standard
deviation of 3.6242 m. Despite a lower mean error compared to the FM
signal interference, the pulse signal interference significantly affects all
three spatial dimensions. This is due to its transient and repetitive
nature which destabilizes signal reception and disrupts GNSS compu­
tations. These findings demonstrate that different types of signal in­
terferences uniquely affect GNSS-based positioning accuracy. The FM
signal interference exerts the most severe impact followed by pulse and
AM signal interference. The elevation direction is the most sensitive to
such disruptions, highlighting the direct influence of signal interference
characteristics on positioning stability in different directions.


_4.2. Positioning performances under different environment scenarios_


Table 5 provides a detailed analysis of the performances of the po­
sitioning system under three distinct environmental scenarios: Open
Area, Mountain Occlusion, and Tunnel. Key metrics of mean error,
standard deviation, and UTM coordinate system components are com­
pared to assess the accuracy and stability of the system. This compar­
ison highlights the impact of different environmental conditions on
positioning performances.
In open areas, it exhibits high precision with a mean error of
1.0279 m and minimal fluctuation, highlighting its robustness under
unobstructed conditions. However, in mountainous environments, the
mean error increases to 1.2979 m, reflecting the effects of signal mul­
tipath and obstructions caused by complex terrain, with the elevation
direction being particularly sensitive to these factors. Tunnel scenarios
show the most pronounced degradation with a mean error of 5.6670 m.
This dramatic increase results from severe signal blockages in tunnels
and the instability during signal reacquisition after exiting the tunnel.
Fig. 10 illustrates the evolution of position error in the tunnel en­
vironment. During the “Inside the Tunnel” phase, GNSS signals are
completely blocked by the surrounding structure, resulting in a loss of
positioning capability (represented by the gray shaded region). Upon
exiting the tunnel (marked by the vertical dashed line), the system
begins to reacquire GNSS signals; however, due to severe multipath
effects and signal obstructions, the initial position errors are sig­
nificantly large. As the train moves further away from the tunnel exit,
the surrounding environment improves, leading to a gradual reduction
in error, which eventually stabilizes. This trend highlights the critical



**Fig. 10.** Position errors in tunnel scenario.


impact of tunnel environments and their transitions on GNSS-based
positioning performance.


**5. Conclusion**


This paper presents a CPNs-based approach to evaluate the perfor­
mances of GNSS-based train positioning systems under real-world op­
erational conditions. By constructing a hierarchical CPN model, the
study successfully models the process of GNSS signal reception and
interferences, as well as the impacts of complex environmental factors
on the GNSS-based train positioning. The simulation results emphasize
that signal interferences (i.e., interfered by AM, FM, and pulse signals)
have a significant impact on the GNSS-based positioning accuracy, with
FM interference exerting the most pronounced effects, particularly in
the vertical direction. Environmental factors like terrain obstructions
and tunnel shielding further degrade signal visibility and increase po­
sitioning errors. The model also identifies critical challenges during
signal reacquisition, particularly in tunnel transitions, which exacerbate
error growth.
While the proposed approach effectively demonstrates the impact of
various signal interferences and environmental conditions on the per­
formance of the GNSS-based train positioning, future research could
explore the integration of additional signal interference mitigation
techniques to improve the accuracy and stability of the GNSS-based
positioning for railway applications.

user_input: Vor welchen Herausforderungen steht der Einsatz von GNSS in der digitalen Schieneninfrastruktur und wie trägt die Integration von Inertialsensoren (INS) dazu bei, einen ausfallsicheren Bahnbetrieb zu gewährleisten?
response:
Die Modernisierung des Schienenverkehrs hin zu einer „digitalen Schiene“ setzt auf eine präzise und kontinuierliche Zugortung, um die Effizienz zu steigern und die Abhängigkeit von teurer Infrastruktur am Gleis (wie Balisen) zu verringern. Das vierte Dokument analysiert hierzu die technologischen Hürden und Lösungen:


1. Die Verwundbarkeit von Satellitensignalen
Ein zentrales Problem ist die schwache Sendeleistung von GNSS-Signalen, wenn sie die Erdoberfläche erreichen (typischerweise zwischen -127 dBm und -130 dBm). Dies macht sie extrem anfällig für:


Unbeabsichtigte Störungen: Funkrauschen aus der Umgebung.



Absichtliche Angriffe (Jamming): Gezielte Störsignale, die den Empfang blockieren und so die Sicherheit des gesamten Zugsteuerungssystems gefährden können.


2. Der Lösungsansatz: Sensor-Fusion zur Resilienz-Steigerung
Um diese Schwachstelle zu schließen, wird das GNSS mit einem Inertialen Navigationssystem (INS) kombiniert. Im Gegensatz zu Satelliten ist ein INS vollkommen autonom, da es die Bewegung des Zuges über interne Sensoren (Beschleunigung und Drehung) misst.



Deeply-coupled Integration: In diesem System sind GNSS und INS nicht nur oberflächlich verbunden; die INS-Daten unterstützen direkt die Signalverarbeitung im GNSS-Empfänger.



Stützfunktion: Selbst wenn das Satellitensignal durch Jamming gestört wird, liefert das INS weiterhin verlässliche Informationen über die Position und Geschwindigkeit des Zuges.


3. Nutzen für die Sicherheit und Wirtschaftlichkeit
Die Integration dieser Technologien führt zu einer deutlich höheren Resilienz (Widerstandsfähigkeit) des Gesamtsystems:


Präzision unter Druck: Experimente zeigen, dass die Positionsfehler selbst bei starken Störangriffen um über 63 % reduziert werden können.



Kontinuität: Das System verhindert einen plötzlichen Abriss der Ortung, was im Ernstfall Notbremsungen oder Betriebsausfälle verhindert.



Infrastruktur-Unabhängigkeit: Durch die höhere Zuverlässigkeit der fahrzeugseitigen Ortung können kostspielige bodengebundene Anlagen sicher reduziert werden

### INS-aided GNSS jamming protection in support of resilient train positioning

Zhuojian Cao [a], Jiang Liu [a][,][b][,][c][,][⁎], Wei Jiang [a][,][b][,][c], Baigen Cai [a][,][b][,][c]

A R T I C L E I N F O


_Keywords:_
GNSS jamming
Deeply-coupled integration
GNSS/INS


**1. Introduction**



A B S T R A C T


Railway safety and efficiency increasingly rely on precise train positioning. The integration of the Global
Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) into railway control systems aims to reduce dependence on track-side in­
frastructure. While GNSS has significantly improved train localization, challenges such as the susceptibility to
jamming remain. To address this, this paper introduces an Inertial Navigation System (INS)-aided train posi­
tioning system based on deep integration, exploring its performance through semi-physical experiments and
simulations. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed solution is able to reduce the positioning error
by 63.47 %, and the velocity error by 58.47 % under jamming conditions. The study highlights the potential of
deep integration for improving the resilience of GNSS-based train control systems, especially in the face of Radio
Frequency (RF) jamming threats.



Railway safety and efficiency depend increasingly on knowing every
train’s position. Traditional train positioning technologies usually in­
volve track circuit positioning, axle counter positioning, and intersec­
tion inductive loop positioning. However, these methods are often
limited by insufficient positioning accuracy and high maintenance
costs. The application of the Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS)
in railway train control systems aims to reduce as much as possible the
use of track-side infrastructures for train location awareness, including
track circuits and Balises. GNSS-based train positioning has been ex­
tensively researched worldwide. Specific projects and initiatives have
been undertaken to integrate GNSS into the railway systems.

Early in the 1990s, the United States introduces the concept of the
Positive Train control System (PTS) [1], which relies on Global Posi­
tioning System (GPS), accelerometers, and fiber-optic gyroscopes for
the train positioning function. By December 2020, the United States
achieved 100 % implementation of Positive Train Control (PTC) across
all required railroads, marking a historic milestone in rail safety and
interoperability [2]. CLUG 2.0 project [3] launched in 2023 in Europe
aims to enhance the European Rail Traffic Management System
(ERTMS) by integrating an on-board GNSS+EGNOS-based multi-sensor
fusion architecture. In China, the China State Railway Group Co., Ltd.
introduced a new train control system, the Chinese Train Control



System-New (CTCS-N) [4], which proposed a “train-borne centric” train
operation control method. This system incorporates on-board GNSS
positioning, speed sensors, and electronic railway maps to achieve train
localization. GNSS has been acknowledged as a pivotal enabler in im­
proving the cost efficiency and automation level of the train control
system.

In complex railway operation scenarios, such as urban canyons,
tunnels, and stations with cover, the limited GNSS signal visibility, and
weak observation geometry would greatly decrease the positioning
performance. Thus, the integration solutions are typically proposed for
the train safety positioning. A typical integration solution for GNSSbased train positioning is based on the data fusion with both GNSS and
Inertial Navigation System (INS) sensors. The GNSS/INS integrated
solution is capable of providing full navigation solutions of position,
velocity, and attitude in high positioning accuracy, high updating rate
even under the GNSS limited scenario when the visible satellites are less
than four. Additionally, there is an increasing concern that GNSS is also
vulnerable to radio frequency jamming, due to the weak signal power of
the GNSS signal. More and more GNSS disturbance events are reported
because of jamming, in worldwide, including maritime applications

[5,6], aviation applications [7], and also railway applications [8,9].
GNSS in-band interferences, for example, intentional jamming shows a
source of potential threats to the safe, secure, and resilient operation of
a dense railway system utilizing GNSS as the main positioning method.

It is of great importance to develop specific countermeasures to protect
train positioning from jamming-caused GNSS disturbance.

Research institutions and GNSS receiver manufacturers have been
developing GNSS receivers equipped with anti-jamming capabilities. A
survey of state-of-art approaches is introduced, based on the functional
stages of the receiver in which they operate. Typically, they could be
implemented at the front-end, pre-correction, or post-correlation stages,
with others functioning at the navigation processing stage. Spatial fil­
tering [9–11], functioning at the front-end stage, uses antenna arrays to
point the receiver antenna beam toward the GNSS satellites and away
from jammers. In the pre-correction stage, time/frequency filtering

[12–14] enables the jamming mitigation before the jamming signal
enters the receiver. Vector tracking is a typical approach that works on
the post-correction stage. Instead of tracking in-view satellites sepa­
rately in the conventional baseband signal processing, vector tracking

[15,16] is achieved through feedback of the receiver states from the
navigation module to the signal tracking channels as prior information
and constraints. Additionally, the inertial-aiding-based strategy at the
navigation processing stage provides a different way. INS is a selfcontained navigation approach in which measurements provided by
accelerometers and gyroscopes are used to track the states relative to a
known starting state. A remarkable advantage of an INS is that it is
inherently immune to Radio Frequency (RF) signals, which makes it
possible to benefit from the mitigation of GNSS jamming signals. In the
railway domain, an extensive analysis of interference events based on a
one-year dataset collected across the Swiss railway network was con­
ducted in Ref. [8], which revealed widespread occurrences of GNSS
interference, particularly clustered around larger cities and densely
populated residential areas. A test and evaluation of GNSS-based
railway positioning under various jamming conditions was presented in
Ref. [17], demonstrating the susceptibility of train localization perfor­
mance to interference. A resilient localization framework operating at
the measurement level, incorporating jamming discrimination and
multi-sensor fusion, was proposed in Ref. [18]. Nevertheless, research
on GNSS anti-jamming solutions for railway environments remains re­
latively limited, which motivates the focus of the present work.

GNSS/INS integration can be categorized into three primary archi­
tectures: loosely coupled, tightly coupled, and deeply coupled sys­
tems [19,20]. In a loosely coupled integration, the GNSS receiver and
INS operate independently, with integration occurring at the navigation
solution level by combining the position and velocity outputs from both
systems. This approach requires a minimum of four visible satellites for
a positioning solution, which can be a limitation in environments with
jamming signals. In contrast, tightly-coupled integration integrates raw
GNSS measurements, such as pseudoranges and Doppler shifts, directly
with INS solution within a filter. This method allows the system to
utilize all available satellite signals, even when fewer than four sa­
tellites are visible, enhancing performance in challenging environ­
ments. Deeply coupled, or ultra-tight, integration goes a step further by



incorporating inertial measurements directly into the GNSS signal
tracking loops. This integration aids in maintaining signal lock under
high dynamics and interference conditions, as the INS can provide
predictive information to the GNSS receiver’s tracking algorithms. The
advantages of deep integration are particularly evident in environments
with significant interference or high dynamics. In Ref. [21], the antijamming performance of the INS velocity aiding tracking loop is eval­
uated and compared, using a continuous wave jamming scenario. While
the superiority of INS-aided deep integration has been preliminarily
validated in jamming scenarios, current research on anti-jamming
methods remains constrained by simple jamming models such as con­
tinuous wave jamming. The mechanisms under different jamming
modulation schemes remain underexplored. This study expands the
exploration of jamming types by focusing on sweep-frequency inter­
ference. Considering the compatibility of the GNSS/INS deeply-coupled
integration with the current GNSS-based train positioning function in
the control system conception, this paper proposes an INS-aided train
determination system (LDS) based on the deep integration. The system
profile and the GNSS jamming signals are briefly introduced firstly.
Then, we give a detailed introduction of deeply-coupled integration
design. Finally, a hardware simulator is used and semi-physical ex­
periments are conducted to assess the proposed system’s performance
with a sweep frequency jamming scenario.


**2. System design**


From Fig. 1, it can be found that multiple information resources are
involved in the train positioning system, where the inertial data from
the accelerometers and gyroscopes is taken into the Position, Velocity,
and Timing (PVT) calculation to enhance the resilience to abnormal
jamming attacks. By integrating an INS directly into the train onboard
subsystem, it becomes unnecessary to install or modify the track-side
subsystem, thereby simplifying system design and maintenance,
thereby providing a flexible architecture for modern rail train control
systems.

From a signal processing perspective, jamming signals are collected
and analyzed together with the authentic GNSS signals by the antenna
and the subsequent front-end stage in the GNSS receiver simulta­
neously. The signal contaminated by jamming signals would directly
affect the subsequent stages of the receiver’s processing chain, in­
cluding acquisition and tracking stages. In the tracking stage, jamming
prevents the code and carrier loops from maintaining stable signal
tracking, ultimately resulting in pronounced errors or even failure in
the PVT solution. In the proposed system, the Doppler shift information
derived from INS is fed back to aid the GNSS tracking loop, improving
the overall resilience of the navigation solution. Inertial mechanization
is used to infer Inertial Solution at discrete epochs, while the GNSS
tracking loop operates simultaneously until they meet the integration
epoch. Then, integrated filter realizes the state estimation based on the

information from both the INS and GNSS pseudorange/pseudorange
rate, where a specific estimator (e.g., nonlinear Kalman filter) would be
adopted. Based on this traditional tightly-coupled GNSS/INS integra­
tion, the estimated navigation states are used to estimate the Doppler
shift information, which is fed back to the GNSS tracking loop to aid the
tracking process. The system operates in a closed-loop way where the
integrated filter continuously updates the system’s state estimation by
combining data from both the GNSS and INS, and the feedback of the
estimated states helps improve the accuracy of both the INS and GNSS
calculations. The INS-aided deep integration system leverages the in­
ertial data, effectively narrowing the bandwidth of the tracking loop,
which helps reduce the noise within the loop and suppress external
jamming signals. The system’s capability to maintain accurate tracking
and position estimation in jamming environments is significantly
strengthened, leading to a more resilient navigation solution.


**3. Jamming analysis and protection**


_3.1. Jamming analysis_


The received signal strength of the GNSS receiver is extremely low
and falls well below the thermal noise floor. Typically, the received GPS
signal levels fall within the range of −127 dBm [22]. The weak char­
acteristics of GNSS signals will lead to an increasing risk of performance
degradation under jamming conditions. Jamming signals may emanate
from telecommunication and electronic systems operating in adjacent
bands or in bands relatively far from the GNSS bands, i.e., Fre­
quency Modulation (FM)/TV transmitter harmonics, Amplitude Mod­
ulation (AM) transmitters, and mobile phone networks. The digital re­
presentation of the received signal in the presence of jamming at the

The jamming signals will not only disrupt the proper functioning of
the receiver’s RF front-end components, such as low-noise amplifiers,
automatic gain control, and analog-to-digital converters, but also ad­
versely affect the operation of the carrier and code tracking loops. This
jamming can prevent the receiver from acquiring and maintaining a
stable lock on the satellite signals, ultimately leading to degraded po­
sitioning performance.


_3.2. INS-aided jamming protection solution_


Fig. 2 illustrates the INS-aided GNSS tracking loop. The carrier
signal measurement enters the tracking loop through the phase dis­
criminator to generate a phase error estimate. This phase error drives
the carrier loop filter which outputs an estimate of the signal Doppler to
drive the carrier controller oscillator (NCO). The NCO duplicates the
phase of the incoming signal, and this phase estimate is fed back to the
phase discriminator. The tracking loop is usually said to be “locked” if
the phase error is less than five degrees.

established by GNSS, which further allows for compensation of the INSinferred results. A 17-dimensional error state vector is defined to

**4. Results and discussion**


In order to analyze the effectiveness of the jamming protection method
under GNSS jamming conditions, further simulations are carried out in a
signal jamming test environment. The setup of the GNSS jamming test
platform is shown in Fig. 4, consisting of several key components, each
connected through specific signal transmission paths, designed to enable
jamming data collection for analytical processing. The Spirent GSS8000
GNSS simulator and the sight N5172B jamming signal generator are con­
trolled from the simulator’s SimGEN host controller, which generates the
required GPS L1 RF signals and the jamming signals, respectively. The
jamming signals are defined, along with all the other scenario parameters,
within the SimGEN. The scenario trajectory collected in the field is input
into the SimGEN software, driving the simulator to generate the corre­
sponding dynamic GNSS RF signals. A signal combiner is utilized to in­
tegrate the clean GPS RF signal and the specific jamming signal. Since the
down-converted Intermediate Frequency (IF) signal is what we need, the
HG-softGPS06 GNSS IF signal collector is connected to the simulated signal
to capture the necessary data, which is then collected and post-processed by
the PC host. Field test results from a train location system were collected
and used as the scenario script to drive the controller. A tactical-grade SPAN
system (a high-precision GNSS/INS integrated system with a tactical grade,
low noise IMU-FSAS) installed on the train was utilized to collect the tra­
jectory and the IMU data.

Two practical local trajectories that last for 160 s are adopted as the
operation script to generate different GNSS jamming tested scenarios
and drive the GNSS simulator. The key parameters of the trajectory are
listed as follows.

**Scenario I:** The trajectory starts at GPS week 2034, second of week
288888, lasting for 160 s. The trajectory replicated a train journey

**Scenario II:** The trajectory starts at GPS week 2034, second of week

In the simulated test, a GNSS signal at GPS L1 frequency is simulated
with a reference level of −130 dBm, while the jamming generator
operates at a center frequency of 1575.42 MHz with a power level of
−90 dBm. The Jamming-to-Signal Ratio (JSR), i.e., the ratio of the
jamming signal power to the GNSS signal power, is used to express the
relationship between GNSS signal power and jamming power. Beyond
complete signal obstruction scenarios, intermediate jamming power
levels can induce significant performance degradation in GNSS re­
ceivers without fully blocking satellite signal acquisition. These inter­
mediate power levels would be hazardous and difficult to detect and
mitigate, making it crucial to accurately assess the impact of jamming
on GNSS signal quality. Previous research [18] has demonstrated sub­
stantial signal quality degradation under jamming conditions with JSR
values ranging from 38 to 54 dB. Accordingly, this study focuses on a
40 dB JSR scenario, corresponding to the specified −130 dBm GPS
signal and −90 dBm jamming power configuration.



A 5 Hz ramp waveform is employed, sweeping ± 1 kHz around the
carrier. The jamming signal for investigating the collected IF signal, and
the software-defined radio GNSS receiver are used to analyze the data.
This software receiver is configured with a second-order phase-locked
loop and a second-order code delay lock loop. The carrier loop band­
width is set to 3 Hz, while the code loop bandwidth is maintained at
3 Hz. In the proposed solution, INS mechanization updates every 1 ms,
while the tracking loop coherent integration time is also set to 1 ms.

Figs. 5 and 6 show the tracking performance of the normal secondorder PLL with an integration time of 1 ms. It can be found that fluc­
tuations of the PLL discriminator values appear in the blue curve be­
cause the normal tracking solution relies solely on the GNSS signals to
determine the receiver’s position and velocity. In the INS-aided tracking
solution, with INS assistance the PLL discriminator exhibits reduced
value fluctuations, achieving a smoother output.

Figs. 7 and 8 illustrate the positioning errors and velocity errors in
three dimensions ( _x_, _y_, _z_ ) for both the normal tracking solution and the
INS-aided solution over 160 s in two scenarios. It can be found in the
figure that the INS-aided solution helps to stabilize these errors, with
the orange curves showing a much smaller variance, which suggests
that the integration of the inertial navigation system provides better
consistency and reduces the impact of GNSS signal irregularities. It can
be seen from the velocity results in Fig. 8, that the proposed solution
keeps the velocity solution well behaved throughout the entire 160 s

accuracy and stability of both positioning and velocity estimation.
The integration of inertial sensors helps to minimize errors caused by

**5. Conclusion**


In this paper, a GNSS jamming protection solution is presented
based on the INS-aided deep integration solution. The solution enables
a self-contained PVT reference that is immune to RF signals. INS in­
formation is sent to the receiver’s baseband, realizing the assistance to
the receiver at the signal processing level. The measured velocity in­
formation from INS is mapped to the LOS between the satellite and the
vehicle to obtain the Doppler of relative motion. The Doppler is fed
back to the NCO in the tracking loop to aid the tracking process. The
evaluation of the proposed solution demonstrates significant



improvements in positioning and velocity, enabling resilient train po­
sitioning for securing next generation GNSS-based train control sys­
tems. Future work will focus on refining mitigation techniques with
extended studies for multi-constellation scenarios (e.g., BeiDou and
Galileo), and exploring field tests in real lines to ensure the resilience of
GNSS-based train localization systems in railway applications.

Der Einsatz von **GNSS in der digitalen Schieneninfrastruktur** eröffnet zahlreiche Vorteile, etwa für die Positionsbestimmung von Zügen in Echtzeit und die Umsetzung eines „zugzentrierten“ Bahnbetriebs. Gleichzeitig bestehen jedoch spezifische Herausforderungen. Zentrale Probleme sind **Signalabschattungen, Multipath-Effekte und atmosphärische Störungen**. In Tunneln, dichten städtischen Gebieten oder engen Schluchten sind direkte Sichtverbindungen zu Satelliten oft nicht verfügbar, was zu temporären Ausfällen oder deutlicher Verschlechterung der Positionsgenauigkeit führt. Auch reflektierte Signale an Gleisanlagen oder Brücken können Messfehler verursachen.

Ein weiteres Problem ist die **Sicherheits- und Zuverlässigkeitsanforderung im Bahnbetrieb**. Anders als im Straßenverkehr darf ein Positionsausfall nicht zu Verzögerungen, falschen Zugabständen oder gar Sicherheitsrisiken führen. GNSS allein kann daher nicht die erforderliche Ausfallsicherheit garantieren.

Hier kommt die **Integration von Inertialsensoren (INS)** ins Spiel. INS-Systeme messen Beschleunigungen und Rotationen des Zuges und können daraus die relative Position über kurze Zeiträume berechnen – selbst wenn GNSS-Signale ausfallen. Die Kombination aus GNSS und INS, oft als **GNSS/INS-Fusion** umgesetzt, ermöglicht die kontinuierliche Positionsbestimmung, wobei GNSS die langfristige Genauigkeit sichert und INS kurzfristige Signalunterbrechungen kompensiert.

Mathematisch erfolgt diese Fusion häufig über **Kalman-Filter**, die GNSS-Messungen und INS-Daten gewichtet und zu einer optimalen Schätzung der Zugposition zusammenführen. Dadurch lässt sich ein **ausfallsicherer Bahnbetrieb** realisieren: Züge können selbst bei GNSS-Ausfällen zuverlässig gesteuert, sichere Abstände eingehalten und virtuelle Blockabschnitte kontinuierlich berechnet werden.

Insgesamt ermöglicht die Kombination von GNSS und INS eine **robuste, präzise und digitale Positionsbestimmung**, die essenziell für moderne, automatisierte und intelligente Schienenbetriebe ist.

user_input: Wie ist der aktuelle Entwicklungsstand von Technologie Radio Block Centre (RBC)
response:
Operativer Status und Komplexität
Einsatz im Realbetrieb: Das untersuchte RBC-System ist bereits betriebsbereit und steuert eine der neu entwickelten italienischen Hochgeschwindigkeitsstrecken zwischen Turin und Novara. Diese Strecke ist seit Februar 2006 in Betrieb.

Software-Umfang: Die Kontrolllogik des RBC ist hochkomplex und umfasst mehr als 250.000 Codezeilen.

Programmiersprache: Die Software ist hauptsächlich in einer proprietären Legacy-Sprache verfasst, die sich in früheren Projekten (insbesondere bei Stellwerken) bewährt hat.

Methodik und technischer Stand
Modellbasierter Ansatz: Der aktuelle Stand der Technik in diesem Kontext ist der Übergang zu modellbasierten Reverse-Engineering-Verfahren unter Verwendung der Unified Modeling Language (UML). Dies ermöglicht es, die Logik komplexer Kontrollsysteme unabhängig von der Syntax der Programmiersprache zu visualisieren.

Objektbasierte Struktur: Obwohl die verwendete Legacy-Sprache keine objektorientierte Syntax besitzt, ist sie „objektbasiert“ aufgebaut, was eine direkte Abbildung von Logikprozessen auf UML-Klassen ermöglicht.

Fortschritte durch Refactoring und Verifizierung
Performance-Steigerung: Durch die Modellierung und anschließendes Refactoring (Code-Umstrukturierung) konnte eine signifikante Leistungssteigerung erzielt werden. Die durchschnittliche Anzahl der Kontrollen wurde minimiert, was die Kapazität des RBC um etwa 30 % der verwaltbaren Züge erhöhte.

Fehlerdiagnose: Der Einsatz von UML-Modellen hat die Effektivität der statischen Verifizierung verdoppelt (im Vergleich zu Projekten ähnlicher Komplexität) und die Zeit für die Fehlerdiagnose während der Testphase verkürzt.

Zukünftige Entwicklungen
Automatisierung: Es wird aktiv an der Automatisierung der Modellkonstruktion gearbeitet, indem die Übersetzungsregeln vom Logikcode in UML-Ansichten formalisiert und in einem automatischen Tool implementiert werden.

Standardisierung: Die systematische Integration dieses Ansatzes in die Standardpraxis des Unternehmens für die Zertifizierung neuer Produkte ist im Gange.
